name: Some subgroups and quotient groups
statement_text:
Let α ∈ G1 , β ∈ G1 , γ ∈ G and δ ∈ G be such that
α = β + Hp and γ = δ + Hp . Then αγ = βδ + Hp .
statement_latex:
\begin{theorem}
Let \(\alpha \in G_1\), \(\beta \in G_1\), \(\gamma \in G\) and \(\delta \in G\) be such that
\[
\alpha = \beta + H_p \quad \text{and} \quad \gamma = \delta + H_p.
\]
Then \(\alpha\gamma = \beta\delta + H_p\).
\end{theorem}
proof_text:
Proof. Two members of G differ by a member of Hp if and only if they take
identical values for any t such that r(t) ≤ p. For any such t, the formula
for (αγ)(t) involves only values of α(u) and γ(u) for r(u) < r(t). Hence,
(αγ)(t) = (βδ)(t).
context_latex:
\paragraph{Context}
Let $G$ be the group (algebra) of elementary weight functions for Runge--Kutta methods.  
Define the linear subspace $H_p = \{\alpha \in G : \alpha(t) = 0 \text{ whenever } r(t) \le p\}$.  
For $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta \in G$, the notation $\alpha = \beta + H_p$ means $\alpha - \beta \in H_p$, i.e., $\alpha$ and $\beta$ agree on trees of order $> p$.

variables.name: G
variables.definition: the group (or algebra) of elementary weight functions for Runge-Kutta methods

variables.name: Hp
variables.definition: linear subspace of G defined by Hp = {α ∈ G : α(t) = 0, whenever r(t) ≤ p}

variables.name: α = β + Hp
variables.definition: means α − β ∈ Hp, i.e., α and β agree up to trees of order > p

preamble: The theorem is about the ideal structure in the group G of elementary weight functions. Hp is an ideal in G, meaning it absorbs multiplication from G. The notation α = β + Hp indicates equivalence modulo Hp.

dependencies.name: One plus Hp is normal in G1
